section.title: The Call of Gideon
section.sectionRationale: After the peace won under Deborah and Barak, Israel again does evil, and Midian crushes them for seven years until they are driven into caves and cry out. Into that ruin God sends first a prophet to name the sin, then the Angel of the LORD to a frightened farmer threshing wheat in a winepress. The chapter is one long call narrative: the summons of the weak, the sign and the altar of peace, the tearing down of Baal, and the two fleeces, all preparing the deliverer through whom God will save.
section.thesis: God raises a deliverer not from the strong but from the fearful and the least, calling a man hiding in a winepress a mighty man of valor, patiently confirming His word by signs, and clothing him with His Spirit, so that when salvation comes it is plainly the LORD's doing and not the arm of the strong.
section.frameNote: Six beats: Midian's oppression, verses 1 to 6; the prophet's rebuke, verses 7 to 10; the Angel of the LORD calls Gideon, verses 11 to 18; the sign and the altar of peace, verses 19 to 24; the altar of Baal torn down, verses 25 to 32; and the Spirit and the two fleeces, verses 33 to 40.

units.label: Midian oppresses Israel
units.note: driven into caves, they cry out

verses.text: Again the Israelites did evil in the sight of the LORD; so He delivered them into the hand of Midian for seven years,
and the hand of Midian prevailed against Israel. Because of the Midianites, the Israelites prepared shelters for themselves in the mountains, caves, and strongholds.
Whenever the Israelites planted their crops, the Midianites, Amalekites, and other people of the east would come up and invade them,
encamping against them as far as Gaza and destroying the produce of the land. They left Israel with no sustenance, neither sheep nor oxen nor donkeys.
For the Midianites came with their livestock and their tents like a great swarm of locusts. They and their camels were innumerable, and they entered the land to ravage it.
Israel was greatly impoverished by Midian, and the Israelites cried out to the LORD.
Now when the Israelites cried out to the LORD because of Midian,
He sent them a prophet, who told them, “This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: I brought you up out of Egypt, out of the house of slavery.
I delivered you out of the hands of Egypt and all your oppressors. I drove them out before you and gave you their land.
And I said to you: ‘I am the LORD your God. You must not fear the gods of the Amorites, in whose land you dwell.’ But you did not obey Me.”

units.label: The Angel of the LORD calls Gideon
units.note: the LORD is with you, O mighty man of valor

verses.text: Then the {{malak|angel}} of the LORD came and sat down under the oak in Ophrah that belonged to Joash the Abiezrite, where his son Gideon was threshing wheat in a winepress to hide it from the Midianites.
And the angel of the LORD appeared to Gideon and said, “The LORD is with you, O {{gibbor|mighty man of valor}}.”

“Please, my Lord,” Gideon replied, “if the LORD is with us, why has all this happened to us? And where are all His wonders of which our fathers told us, saying, ‘Has not the LORD brought us up out of Egypt?’ But now the LORD has forsaken us and delivered us into the hand of Midian.”
The LORD turned to him and said, “Go in the strength you have and {{yasha|save}} Israel from the hand of Midian. Am I not sending you?”
“Please, my Lord,” Gideon replied, “how can I save Israel? Indeed, my clan is the weakest in Manasseh, and I am the youngest in my father’s house.”
“Surely I will be with you,” the LORD replied, “and you will strike down all the Midianites as one man.”
Gideon answered, “If I have found favor in Your sight, give me a sign that it is You speaking with me.
Please do not depart from this place until I return to You. Let me bring my offering and set it before You.” And the LORD said, “I will stay until you return.”

units.label: The sign and the altar of peace

verses.text: So Gideon went in and prepared a young goat and unleavened bread and an ephah of flour. He placed the meat in a basket and the broth in a pot and brought them out to present to Him under the oak.
And the angel of God said to him, “Take the meat and the unleavened bread, lay them on this rock, and pour out the broth.” And Gideon did so.

Then the angel of the LORD extended the tip of the staff that was in his hand and touched the meat and the unleavened bread. And fire flared from the rock and consumed the meat and the unleavened bread. Then the angel of the LORD vanished from his sight.
When Gideon realized that it was the angel of the LORD, he said, “Oh no, Lord GOD! I have seen the angel of the LORD face to face!”
But the LORD said to him, “{{shalom|Peace}} be with you. Do not be afraid, for you will not die.”
So Gideon built an altar to the LORD there and called it The LORD Is Peace. To this day it stands in Ophrah of the Abiezrites.

units.label: The altar of Baal torn down

verses.text: On that very night the LORD said to Gideon, “Take your father’s young bull and a second bull seven years old, tear down your father’s altar to Baal, and cut down the Asherah pole beside it.
Then build a proper altar to the LORD your God on the top of this stronghold. And with the wood of the Asherah pole you cut down, take the second bull and offer it as a burnt offering.”
So Gideon took ten of his servants and did as the LORD had told him. But because he was too afraid of his father’s household and the men of the city, he did it by night rather than in the daytime.
When the men of the city got up in the morning, there was Baal’s altar torn down, with the Asherah pole cut down beside it and the second bull offered up on the newly built altar.
“Who did this?” they said to one another. And after they had investigated thoroughly, they were told, “Gideon son of Joash did it.”
Then the men of the city said to Joash, “Bring out your son. He must die, because he has torn down Baal’s altar and cut down the Asherah pole beside it.”

But Joash said to all who stood against him, “Are you contending for Baal? Are you trying to save him? Whoever pleads his case will be put to death by morning! If Baal is a god, let him contend for himself with the one who has torn down his altar.”
So on that day Gideon was called Jerubbaal, that is to say, “Let Baal contend with him,” because he had torn down Baal’s altar.

units.label: The Spirit and the two fleeces
units.note: clothed with the Spirit, yet asking for signs

verses.text: Then all the Midianites, Amalekites, and other people of the east gathered together, crossed over the Jordan, and camped in the Valley of Jezreel.
So the {{ruach|Spirit}} of the LORD came upon Gideon, who blew the ram’s horn and rallied the Abiezrites behind him.
Calling them to arms, Gideon sent messengers throughout Manasseh, as well as Asher, Zebulun, and Naphtali, so that they came up to meet him.
Then Gideon said to God, “If You are going to save Israel by my hand, as You have said,

then behold, I will place a fleece of wool on the threshing floor. If there is dew only on the fleece and all the ground is dry, then I will know that You are going to save Israel by my hand, as You have said.”
And that is what happened. When Gideon arose the next morning, he squeezed the fleece and wrung out the dew, a bowlful of water.
Then Gideon said to God, “Do not be angry with me; let me speak one more time. Please allow me one more test with the fleece. This time let it be dry, and the ground covered with dew.”
And that night God did so. Only the fleece was dry, and dew covered the ground.

malak.gloss: Messenger or angel. The Angel of the LORD who comes to Gideon speaks as the LORD in the first person and receives worship, and Gideon fears he will die for having seen God face to face. Many read this as a preincarnate appearing of the Son, the LORD Himself present as His own Messenger to call and commission His servant.

gibbor.gloss: A mighty man, warrior, or hero of valor. The Angel greets a frightened farmer hiding in a winepress as a mighty man of valor. The title is not flattery about who Gideon is but a promise about what God's presence will make him; the LORD names the future He will create.

yasha.gloss: To save, deliver, rescue. God commissions Gideon to save Israel from Midian, yet the whole account insists the saving is God's, done through a weak and hesitant man. The root lies behind the names Joshua and Jesus, the LORD who saves; every human deliverer points to the one true Saviour.

ruach.gloss: Spirit, wind, or breath. The Spirit of the LORD clothes Gideon, literally, and he blows the horn to rally Israel. The Spirit is the source of his courage and strength; the deliverance flows not from Gideon's resolve but from God's Spirit taking hold of him.

shalom.gloss: Peace, wholeness, well-being. When Gideon fears death for seeing the Angel, the LORD speaks peace, and Gideon names the altar The LORD Is Peace, YHWH Shalom. It is peace given by God to a trembling man before any battle is fought, grounded in God's presence rather than in circumstances.

basic.context: Israel has sinned again, and for seven years Midian sweeps in like locusts at harvest, stripping the land bare and driving God's people into caves. When they finally cry out, God first sends a prophet to remind them their trouble flows from their own disobedience. Then the Angel of the LORD comes to Gideon, a fearful young man secretly threshing wheat in a winepress, and greets him as a mighty man of valor. Gideon argues, asks for a sign, is given a miraculous fire, and builds an altar he calls The LORD Is Peace. That night he obeys the frightening command to tear down his father's altar to Baal, earning the name Jerubbaal, let Baal contend. When the enemy masses, the Spirit of the LORD clothes Gideon, and he rallies Israel, though he still asks God twice for a sign with a fleece.
basic.hermeneutics: This is the beginning of a call narrative, and its point is not Gideon's greatness but God's grace toward the weak and afraid. The story describes Gideon; it does not always commend him. The fleece, in particular, is Gideon's request for reassurance after God has already spoken plainly and even sent His Spirit; it is not offered to us as a formula for finding God's will. What abides is the God who saves through the least so that the glory is His.
basic.originalLanguages: The malak, the Angel of the LORD, speaks as God Himself and is worshiped. He calls Gideon a gibbor, a mighty man of valor, a promise more than a description. Gideon is sent to yasha, to save, Israel, yet the saving is God's. God speaks shalom, peace, to the trembling man, who names his altar The LORD Is Peace. And when war comes, the ruach, the Spirit of the LORD, clothes Gideon for the task.
basic.typology: The Angel of the LORD who appears, speaks as God, and receives worship foreshadows the Son, God come near to call and save. Gideon, the unlikely deliverer raised up by God's Spirit, is a faint shadow of the true Deliverer. And the altar named The LORD Is Peace anticipates the peace with God that Christ Himself would make, not with the blood of bulls but with His own.
basic.christ: Gideon is a small and flawed picture of the great Saviour to come. God calls the weakest to save Israel, foreshadowing Christ, who saves not by worldly might but in weakness, through the cross. The Angel of the LORD, worshiped and speaking as God, points to the eternal Son. And the altar The LORD Is Peace looks ahead to Jesus, who is our peace, reconciling us to God so that we, like Gideon, may hear do not be afraid.
basic.god: God is patient and gracious with the fearful. He sends a prophet before He sends a rescuer, so Israel knows their trouble is their own doing. He meets Gideon's doubts with signs rather than rejection, speaks peace to his terror, and clothes him with His Spirit. Yet He is also holy and jealous, commanding that Baal's altar be torn down. The whole chapter shows a God who saves the undeserving and does it through the least, so the glory is His.
basic.crossReferences: First Corinthians 1 says God chose the weak and foolish things to shame the strong. Exodus 3 shows a similar call and the same objection, who am I. Ephesians 2 declares that Christ Himself is our peace. And Hebrews 11 lists Gideon among those who through faith became mighty in war.
basic.oneStory: In the dark cycle of the judges, God again hears His people's cry and raises a deliverer, this time a fearful farmer whom He names a mighty man of valor. The call of Gideon is one more rescue in the long story of a God who saves the weak, and it points beyond Gideon to the true Deliverer, Jesus, who is our peace and who saves not by might but by laying down His life.

context.literary: Chapter 6 opens the Gideon narrative, the longest in the book, and follows the settled pattern of Judges: Israel sins, is oppressed, cries out, and God raises a deliverer. But the pattern is stretched and deepened here. Before the rescuer comes a prophet, and the call itself is drawn out in dialogue full of Gideon's objections and requests for signs. The threshing in a winepress, the fire from the rock, the torn-down altar, and the two fleeces are vivid scenes that build the portrait of a hesitant man being formed into a deliverer.
context.historical: The Midianites were nomadic raiders from east of the Jordan who, with the Amalekites and other eastern peoples, swept in on camels at harvest time to plunder Israel's crops and herds. Their seven-year oppression reduced Israel to hiding in mountain caves and threshing grain in hidden winepresses rather than open threshing floors. Baal was the Canaanite storm and fertility god, and the Asherah pole a wooden symbol of his consort; that Gideon's own father maintained such an altar shows how deeply Israel had turned from the LORD.
context.canonical: Gideon belongs to the downward spiral of Judges, where each deliverer is more compromised than the last, exposing Israel's need for a king and, beyond every judge, for the true Saviour. The Angel of the LORD who appears and is worshiped stands in a line of such appearings from Genesis onward. Hebrews 11 remembers Gideon among the faithful, and the whole account anticipates the New Testament truth that God saves through weakness and gives peace through Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that Israel's misery is the fruit of its own disobedience, and that God nonetheless saves, raising a deliverer from the weakest clan and the youngest son, patiently confirming His word, so that the coming victory is unmistakably the LORD's and not the achievement of a strong man.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes Gideon's fear, his repeated requests for signs, and the fleece; it does not prescribe them as models. The fleece is not a method for discerning God's will, since God had already spoken clearly and even sent His Spirit. What is prescriptive is the call to trust and obey the God who saves the weak, and to tear down the idols He commands us to remove.

debates.question: Is the fleece a model for seeking God's guidance, or a sign of weak faith?

views.label: A humble seeking of confirmation
views.case: Gideon faces an overwhelming enemy and asks God to confirm the promise he has already been given; God graciously answers, and Scripture never rebukes him, so the fleece can be read as sincere, if halting, faith reaching for assurance.

views.label: A sign of weak and hesitant faith
views.case: God had already spoken plainly, given a fire from the rock, and even clothed Gideon with His Spirit, verse 34; asking twice more, do not be angry, let me speak one more time, betrays lingering doubt rather than exemplary faith, and the text records it without commending it.

debates.bearing: Either way, the fleece is not offered as a formula for guidance. It shows God's patience with a fearful servant, and it warns us against demanding signs when God has already spoken; we walk by His revealed word, not by rigged tests.

debates.question: Who is the Angel of the LORD in this chapter?

views.label: A created angelic messenger
views.case: The word malak means messenger, and God often sends angels to speak for Him; on this reading the Angel conveys God's words as His representative without being God Himself.

views.label: The LORD Himself appearing, a preincarnate Christ
views.case: The Angel speaks as God in the first person, the text slides between the Angel and the LORD, He receives worship, and Gideon fears death for seeing God face to face; many therefore read this as the LORD Himself present as His own Messenger, an appearing of the eternal Son.

debates.bearing: The account clearly identifies the visitor with the LORD, so the deliverance is rooted in God's own personal presence and points ahead to the Son who would come in the flesh to save.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's key words carry its theology. The visitor is the malak, the Angel of the LORD, yet He speaks as God in the first person, receives Gideon's offering with fire from the rock, and is worshiped; Gideon expects to die because he has seen God face to face. To this fearful man the Angel says, the LORD is with you, O gibbor, mighty man of valor, a title that describes not who Gideon is but who God's presence will make him. Gideon is commissioned to yasha, to save, Israel, though the whole story insists the saving is God's own work through a weak instrument; the same root stands behind the names Joshua and Jesus, the LORD who saves. When terror grips him after the vision, the LORD speaks shalom, peace, and Gideon builds an altar he names The LORD Is Peace, YHWH Shalom, peace given by God before a single blow is struck. And when the enemy masses in the valley, the ruach, the Spirit of the LORD, clothes Gideon, and he sounds the horn; his courage and strength are not his own but the Spirit's. Angel, mighty man, salvation, peace, and Spirit, the very words announce that this rescue belongs to the LORD.

typology.type: The Angel of the LORD who speaks as God and is worshiped
typology.fulfillment: The eternal Son, God come near to call and save His people, who would take flesh in Christ, John 1:14 and 8:58.

typology.type: Gideon, the weak deliverer raised up and empowered by God's Spirit
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true Deliverer, who saves not by worldly might but in weakness through the cross, 1 Corinthians 1:25-27 and 2 Corinthians 13:4.

typology.type: The altar named The LORD Is Peace
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who is our peace, making peace with God by the blood of His cross, Ephesians 2:14 and Colossians 1:20.

study.christ: Gideon is a small, flawed foreshadowing of the great Saviour. God deliberately chooses the weakest clan and the youngest son, the man hiding in a winepress, to save Israel, so that when victory comes no one can boast; this is the same wisdom by which God saves the world through the apparent weakness of the cross, 1 Corinthians 1:25-27. The Angel of the LORD, who speaks as God, receives worship, and is called the LORD, points ahead to the eternal Son who would come in the flesh. And the altar Gideon builds, The LORD Is Peace, anticipates the deeper peace Jesus secures: not a truce declared before battle but reconciliation with God won through His own blood, so that He Himself is our peace, Ephesians 2:14. To every trembling sinner who has met the holy God, the risen Christ still says what the LORD said to Gideon, do not be afraid.
study.god: The God of this chapter is patient, gracious, and holy. He is patient, meeting Gideon's doubts with signs rather than rejection and bearing with his fearful requests. He is gracious, hearing a people whose misery is their own fault, sending first a word of truth through the prophet and then a rescuer, and speaking peace to a terrified man. He is holy and jealous, tolerating no rival and commanding that Baal's altar and the Asherah pole be torn down. And He is sovereign, raising a deliverer from the least and clothing him with His Spirit, so that the salvation to come will plainly be His doing. He saves the undeserving through the weak, that all the glory might return to Him.

crossReferences.note: God chooses the weak and foolish things of the world to shame the strong, so no one may boast, the very principle at work in Gideon's call.
Moses too objects, who am I, and receives the same answer, I will be with you, the pattern of God's call to reluctant servants.
Christ Himself is our peace, reconciling us to God, the fullness of what Gideon's altar The LORD Is Peace foreshadowed.
Gideon is named among those who through faith were made strong out of weakness and became mighty in war.

study.oneStory: In the darkening cycle of the judges, Israel sins, Midian oppresses, and the people cry out; and once more God hears and raises a deliverer, this time a fearful farmer He names a mighty man of valor. The call of Gideon shows a God who saves the weak so the glory is His, who speaks peace to the trembling and clothes them with His Spirit. It is one more rescue in the long story that runs toward the true Deliverer, Jesus, God's own Angel and Son, who is our peace and who saves the world not by might but by the weakness of the cross.

quiz.q: Where was Gideon when the Angel of the LORD came to him, and how did the Angel greet him?

options.text: Hiding and threshing wheat in a winepress, greeted as a mighty man of valor.
options.note: Verses 11-12 show God naming the fearful farmer by the future His presence would create.

options.text: Threshing wheat secretly, and told the LORD was with him.
options.note: True as far as it goes, but it omits the striking title, mighty man of valor.

options.text: Leading Israel's army, and hailed as a proven warrior.
options.note: Gideon was hiding from Midian, not leading an army; the title was promise, not record.

options.text: Worshiping at the altar of the LORD, and commended for his faith.
options.note: He was hiding grain in a winepress, and his father's household kept an altar to Baal.

quiz.q: Why did God send a prophet before He sent a deliverer?

options.text: To remind Israel that their oppression flowed from their own disobedience to the LORD.
options.note: Verses 8-10 name the sin before grace is shown, so the rescue is understood rightly.

options.text: To announce that Gideon would be the one to save them.
options.note: The prophet named their sin; he did not announce Gideon, who is called afterward.

options.text: To promise deliverance with no mention of their sin.
options.note: The prophet's whole message centered on their disobedience, but you did not obey Me.

options.text: To tell Israel that God had abandoned them for good.
options.note: God had not abandoned them; He was about to raise a deliverer.

quiz.q: What is the significance of Gideon tearing down his father's altar to Baal?

options.text: True deliverance begins with removing the idol; the LORD alone must be worshiped, and Baal cannot defend himself.
options.note: Verses 25-32 show the war against Midian begins with war on the idol at home.

options.text: Gideon proved his courage by acting against Baal, though he did it at night.
options.note: He did obey, but the deeper point is the LORD's exclusive claim, not merely Gideon's courage.

options.text: It shows Gideon disliked his father's religion for personal reasons.
options.note: The command came from the LORD and concerned true worship, not personal preference.

options.text: It was an act of rebellion the LORD had not authorized.
options.note: The LORD Himself commanded it that very night, verse 25.

quiz.q: How should we understand Gideon's request for the fleece?

options.text: It shows God's patience with a fearful servant; it is not a formula for us to seek guidance.
options.note: God had already spoken and sent His Spirit; the fleece is described, not prescribed.

options.text: It was a sincere reach for reassurance that God graciously answered.
options.note: True and kindly put, but it understates that God had already given clear word and His Spirit.

options.text: It is the Bible's recommended way to discover God's will in decisions.
options.note: This misreads a described episode as a prescribed method; the text offers no such formula.

options.text: It proves Gideon had strong, exemplary faith.
options.note: Asking twice more after clear word and the Spirit reflects lingering doubt, not exemplary faith.

quiz.q: What does the name Gideon gave his altar, The LORD Is Peace, point to?

options.text: God's gift of peace to a trembling man, foreshadowing the peace with God that Christ secures.
options.note: Peace is spoken before any battle; it anticipates Christ our peace, Ephesians 2:14.

options.text: The peace Israel would enjoy once Midian was finally defeated.
options.note: Peace did follow, but the altar marks peace God gave Gideon before the battle, not merely after it.

options.text: A hope that God would keep Israel from all future conflict.
options.note: The name marks God's present peace to Gideon, not a promise of no further conflict.

options.text: Gideon's own confidence that he could win the war.
options.note: The peace came from God's word, not from Gideon's self-confidence.

quiz.q: What enabled Gideon to rally Israel against Midian?

options.text: The Spirit of the LORD clothed him, so his courage and strength came from God.
options.note: Verse 34 traces the deliverance to God's Spirit, not to Gideon's resolve.

options.text: He blew the ram's horn and gathered the tribes to himself.
options.note: He did sound the horn, but the text says the Spirit came upon him first.

options.text: His growing self-confidence after seeing the miraculous fire.
options.note: The account credits the Spirit of the LORD, not Gideon's confidence.

options.text: The size and strength of his own clan in Manasseh.
options.note: Gideon called his clan the weakest and himself the youngest; strength was God's gift.

questions: God greeted a man hiding in a winepress as a mighty man of valor, naming the future His presence would make. Where might God be calling you according to who He will make you, rather than who you feel you are?
Before sending a rescuer, God sent a prophet to name Israel's sin honestly. Is there a place where you need to hear the truth about your own disobedience before you can receive God's help?
Gideon's first act of deliverance was tearing down the idol in his own family. What idol is God calling you to remove at home before anything else?
God spoke peace to Gideon while he trembled, before any battle was fought. How does it change your fear to know that in Christ, God speaks peace to you now, not only after your struggles are over?

The Spirit of the LORD, not Gideon's resolve, was the source of his courage. Where do you need to stop relying on your own strength and ask God's Spirit to clothe you for what He has called you to do?

translationNotes.note: Angel renders the Hebrew malak, messenger; the BSB footnote notes it may be capitalized as Angel, since throughout the passage the figure speaks as the LORD Himself and is worshiped.
The LORD turned to him; the BSB footnote records that the Septuagint reads the Angel of the LORD, reflecting how closely the Angel and the LORD are identified in this account.
The LORD Is Peace renders the Hebrew YHWH Shalom, the name Gideon gave the altar to mark the peace God spoke to him.
Jerubbaal probably means let Baal contend; the name became a lasting testimony that Baal could not even defend his own altar.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Judges 6:1-40. One em dash in verse 38 (the dew, a bowlful of water) was converted to a comma; no em or en dashes present anywhere in the file.
